prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

4. Shows genuine reflection and growth
5. The ending insight about "failure is just information" is mature
6. Natural voice - sounds like an authentic teenager/young person
7. The transition to debate team shows adaptability

**What doesn't work:**
1. Writing is quite plain/conversational - lacks stylistic sophistication for a competitive writing program
2. No imagery or sensory details - we don't see, hear, or feel the gym
3. Sentence structure is very simple and repetitive (lots of short, declarative sentences)
4. Tells rather than shows in many places - "I was really upset" rather than showing the upset
5. Some clichéd phrasing ("out of my league," "in over my head")
6. The reflection, while solid, is somewhat surface-level and predictable
7. Missing opportunities for deeper introspection or more creative narrative techniques
8. The transition to debate is mentioned but not developed - feels rushed
9. No real scene-setting or atmospheric writing
10. The opening is generic ("The first time I really failed...")

**For a competitive writing program:**
This is problematic. The content and character development are fine for a general college essay, but for a writing program specifically, they'll be looking for:
- Distinctive voice and style
- Sophisticated sentence variety
- Vivid, specific details
- Creative structure or approach
- Evidence of craft and technique
- More "showing" than "telling"

This reads more like a competent first draft than polished creative writing.

The real issue is that admissions committees for writing programs prioritize technical skill and artistic execution alongside personal narrative. The essay demonstrates self-awareness and character, but lacks the linguistic precision and creative sophistication they're seeking.

# Grade: **5.5/10**

## What Works:
- **Honest and relatable** - The vulnerability feels genuine, not manufactured
- **Clear structure** - Addresses the prompt directly with setup, failure, and meaningful reflection
- **Specific details** - The "stay out of the way" comment and the revelation about Marcus are effective moments
- **Mature insights** - The final point about "failure is just information" and "you don't know what you don't know" shows real reflection
- **Natural voice** - Sounds authentic, not overly formal

## What Doesn't Work (Critical for a Writing Program):
- **Lacks stylistic sophistication** - The writing is very plain and conversational. For a competitive *writing* program, this needed to demonstrate craft
- **Too much telling, not enough showing** - "I was really upset" rather than showing us that emotion through detail and scene
- **Weak imagery** - No sensory details. We never see the gym, smell the floor wax, hear the squeaking shoes, feel the frustration physically
- **Repetitive sentence structure** - Mostly simple, short declarative sentences. Needs more variety and complexity
- **Generic opening** - "The first time I really failed..." is a missed opportunity for a compelling hook
- **Clichéd language** - "out of my league," "in over my head," "it worked out fine"
- **Underdeveloped ending** - The debate team pivot is interesting but feels rushed

## Bottom Line:
This would be a decent personal statement for general college admission, but for a **competitive writing program**, it doesn't showcase enough technical skill or creative flair. The student can reflect, but they haven't demonstrated they can *write* at a high level.

I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. He's hoping to get into a competitive writing program with something like this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.
